posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
An era is passing as time does inevitably does its thing.

Who are the old guard still out there forging on?

Of course winemakers, but really could be any level of the business that may be of interest and recognition.

Creating a thread I wish I could have read 20 years ago!

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Never think of him as old, but Joel Peterson is still at it.

posts.name: CJ Beazley
posts.cooked: Dave Ramey maybe?

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Certainly a poor choice for a title on my part…

maybe longest tenured?

posts.name: Stephen Rash
posts.cooked: Cathy Corison

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: So you mean at a single winery the whole time?

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: TomHill

posts.name: Ed Steinway
posts.cooked: Ric Forman?

Uhhhh… uncalled for, Karl. You must respect your elders!! I’m only 82… figure I have another 20 productive yrs left in me!!
Tom

When I visited Piedmont in 2003, I got to meet a number of the old guard, many of whom have past:

Bartolo Mascarello
Bruno Giacosa
Guiseppe Rinadi
Francesco Rinadi (?)
Beppe Colla
Giacomo Oddero
Olivio and Gildo Cavallotto
Teobaldo Cappellano

Two who are still fighting the good fight are:

Mauro (Guiseppe) Mascarello
and
Lorenzo Accomasso who seemed 100 years old even then!

TomHill:

I’m only 82… figure I have another 20 productive yrs left in me!!

The Dalai Lama just turned 90 and said he hopes to live to 130. Why sell yourself short?

posts.name: W Chris Withelder
posts.cooked: I just had a similar talk with a friend a week ago. Who still seeks out those legacy brands; Sterling, Markham, Simi, Jordan, BV, Guenoc, and the list goes on. I’m sure none of these make bad wine but the new, sexy and trendy phase is long gone. Some that I mentioned may be long gone already or scooped up by corporate regimes or already gone. I just don’t see people being giddy to get in a Cakebread, Stags Leap, or ZD wine club.

posts.name: Bruce Klen
posts.cooked: I wonder if any folks on WB are 85+??

posts.name: Gary Schulte
posts.cooked: For east coast representatives I would say the Massoud family at Paumanok on the North Fork of Long Island NY and Jim Law of Linden Vineyards in Virginia. Both have had significant influence on the east coast wine industry. And both have staying power and have been going for more than 40 years. Congrats to both !!!

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked:
Meant with all respect!!

20+ and then some!!!

Paul is retired. Just a figurehead now. Obviously a living icon.

While maybe not involved on a daily basis now, I understand that he still takes an active part in the Monte Bello assemblage process and maybe others over the course of the year. He also clearly fits what the OP was looking for …

“Of course winemakers, but really could be any level of the business that may be of interest and recognition.”

Scott_Jameson:
Wchrisw13:

Paul is retired. Just a figurehead now. Obviously a living icon.

While maybe not involved on a daily basis now, I understand that he still takes an active part in the Monte Bello assemblage process and maybe others over the course of the year. He also clearly fits what the OP was looking for …

“Of course winemakers, but really could be any level of the business that may be of interest and recognition.”

Jeffrey Patterson passed the reins to Austin Chin, but is still very involved at Mount Eden.

Bob Mullen founded Woodside Vineyards in 1962 and is still involved.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked: René Rostaing, who was lovely when I met him briefly, seems fiarly up there in age, especially for someone who still goes out in those super steep Cote Rotie vineyards.

posts.name: Kevin Diffley
posts.cooked:
Just of those I visited a couple weeks ago…
Renato Cigliuti is in his 80’s still in the vines everyday according to Claudia.

And Federico Oberto—the father of Nicola Oberto of Trediberri is still extremely active in their process according to Nic. But he was at Renato Ratti until 2005.
